summary: DHH explains why 37signals abandoned their initial plan to build ONCE/Campfire's authentication entirely around passkeys. Despite investing significant engineering effort into a passkey-based system, they ripped it all out due to poor user experience. The core problem is that passkeys are tied to specific platforms and devices in ways users don't understand, creating lockout scenarios worse than traditional passwords. He argues that passkeys are essentially a halfway house to password managers but with less portability. Instead, they're sticking with passwords plus email-based second-factor authentication on first login from new devices. He concludes that passkeys aren't worth the implementation complexity or the UX pitfalls they introduce.
key_points: Passkeys are platform-locked in non-obvious ways, easily leaving users unable to access accounts across devices and operating systems
The cross-device solutions like QR code scanning are cumbersome and alien to most users
If you have to educate users about passkey pitfalls, you might as well teach them to use a cross-platform password manager instead
Email as a second factor on first login from a new device is a simpler, more universally understood alternative
Having a real problem (password reuse) doesn't mean every proposed solution is actually better
money_quotes: The problem with passkeys is that they're essentially a halfway house to a password manager, but tied to a specific platform in ways that aren't obvious to a user at all.

If you're going to teach someone how to deal with all of this, and all the potential pitfalls that might lock them out of your service, you almost might as well teach them how to use a cross-platform password manager like 1password.
But just because we have a real problem doesn't mean that all proposed solutions are actually going to be better.
Unless you accept the idea that all your passwords should be tied to one computing ecosystem, and thus make it hard to use alternative computers.
Passkeys seemed promising, but not all good intentions result in good solutions.
I'm disappointed to report that passkeys don't appear worth the complexity of implementation (which is substantial!) nor the complexity and gotchas of the user experience.
key_insight: Passkeys solve a real problem but create worse ones by locking authentication to specific platforms and devices, making a good password manager the better practical choice.
